name: Brooklyn Bagels
recipeIngredient: 1 packet active dry yeast (or 1 scant tablespoon)
1/2 cup all-purpose flour, unbleached (1/2 to 1) (see note)
recipeInstructions:
1. In a large bowl, mix together the first three ingredients.
2. Soften the yeast in a small bowl with 1/4 cup warm water.
3. Dissolve the malt extract in 1 cup hot water and let it cool to warm.
4. Make a well in the center of the flour mixture. Pour in the softened yeast, malt water, and dissolve malt extract mixture.
5. Stir until smooth. Beat the dough vigorously to develop gluten.
6. Gradually add the flour until a very stiff dough forms.
7. Turn out onto a floured surface and knead for 10 minutes.
8. Divide the dough into 12 equal pieces. Shape each piece into a ball.
9. Cover balls with a cloth and work with one at a time.
10. Place the ball on a lightly floured surface. Flatten slightly.
11. Poke the center with a forefinger, going all the way through.
12. Gently open up the dough to form a ring, keeping it symmetrical. The hole should be about one-third of the bagel's diameter.
13. Place shaped rings on a non-stick baking sheet. Cover and let rise for 15-20 minutes until they just double in size.
14. Meanwhile, prepare the boiling liquid by heating water in a large pot.
15. Stir in malt barley extract and bring to a boil.
16. Reduce heat to maintain a gentle simmer.
17. Preheat oven to 475°F (245°C).
18. Place risen bagels into the simmering water one at a time; they should float when ready.
19. After about 30 seconds, flip them so they remain submerged for another minute in total.
20. Simmer several at a time without overcrowding the pot.
21. Remove with a skimmer and place on a non-stick baking sheet, spacing them apart.
22. Brush each bagel with an egg wash (1 egg beaten with 1 tablespoon water).
23. Optionally, sprinkle with coarse salt or poppy seeds.
24. Bake at 475°F (245°C) for 10-12 minutes until well-browned.
25. Cool slightly on wire racks.
26. Serve warm.
Note: In place of unbleached white flour, equal parts of stone-ground rye flour, stone-ground whole-wheat flour, and unbleached all-purpose flour may be used.
